resources.string: AnyCheck
Root Detection Analyzer
Start Detection
Re-detect
Detecting\u2026
Detection Complete
Summary
Total Checks
Issues Found
Safe Items
Not Run
Reset
Back
Retry
Detection Failed
Detection failed: %1$s
Unknown error
Device Appears Clean
Root May Possibly Exist
Root Framework Detected
Detection complete \u00b7 %1$d checks run
Detected
Safe
Errors
CLEAN
Reason
Solution
Technical Details
Risk Level
Category
Collapse
Expand
Detected
Not Detected
Error
Checking
Critical
High
Medium
Low
Info
Confirmed root framework core components present
Strong indication of root framework presence
Root may exist, needs further confirmation
Low risk indicator, usually doesn\'t affect security verdict
Magisk
KernelSU
APatch
SU Binary
Root Management
Root
Xposed
System
ADB/Debug
Frida
Environment
Network
All
Detected Only
Magisk
KernelSU
All (%1$d)
Detected (%1$d)
Magisk (%1$d)
KernelSU (%1$d)
APatch (%1$d)
SU Binary (%1$d)
Xposed (%1$d)
Magisk detection (17 checks)
KernelSU detection (8 checks)
APatch & generic root (5 checks)
Xposed / LSPosed hook detection (13 checks)
Advanced root checks (19 checks)
Extra: mounts, capabilities, bypass modules (15 checks)
AnyCheck detects root management frameworks (Magisk, KernelSU, APatch) using multiple detection vectors.
For security research purposes only.
\u26a0\ufe0f For security research purposes only.
Copy Results
Results copied to clipboard
About
Version
Version %1$s
About App
AnyCheck is a comprehensive Android root detection tool that identifies Magisk, KernelSU, APatch and other root management frameworks, as well as Xposed/LSPosed hook frameworks.
Detection Methods
Magisk Detection
17 checks
KernelSU Detection
8 checks
APatch & Generic Root
5 checks
Xposed / LSPosed
13 checks
Advanced Root Checks
19 checks
Luna Methods
22 checks
System Integrity
Multiple checks
Risk Levels
Note: When only MEDIUM or lower risk items are detected, the summary shows \u201cRoot May Possibly Exist\u201d instead of \u201cRoot Framework Detected\u201d.
Initializing checks\u2026
Magisk checks complete
KernelSU checks complete
Generic checks complete
Xposed checks complete
Advanced checks complete
Extra checks complete
Luna checks complete
Reveny-inspired checks complete
RootBeerFresh checks complete
Ruru-inspired checks complete
System integrity checks complete
No action required.
KernelSU Files Detected
KernelSU Files
KernelSU-specific files or directories found.
No KernelSU-specific files detected.
The following KernelSU files were found: %1$s. KernelSU is a kernel-based root solution. ksud is the KernelSU daemon and /data/adb/ksu is its working directory.
No known KernelSU file paths were found on this device.
To remove KernelSU: Use KernelSU Manager → Uninstall, or flash a non-KernelSU kernel/stock boot image via fastboot.
KernelSU Manager Detected
KernelSU Manager Packages
KernelSU Manager or related apps installed.
No KernelSU Manager packages found.
Found KernelSU packages: %1$s. The KernelSU Manager app is the management interface for KernelSU. Its presence confirms KernelSU is installed.
No known KernelSU package names were installed.
Use KernelSU Manager to uninstall, then flash a stock kernel.
KernelSU in Kernel Version
KernelSU Kernel Version
KernelSU signature found in kernel version string.
No KernelSU signature in kernel version string.
Could not read kernel version.
The kernel version string contains KernelSU markers: %1$s. KernelSU patches the kernel and embeds its version in the kernel string. Kernel: %2$s
The kernel version string does not contain KernelSU markers.
Failed to read /proc/version: %1$s
Flash a stock (non-KernelSU) kernel via fastboot or recovery.
Ensure /proc/version is accessible.
KernelSU Database Found
KernelSU Database
KernelSU SQLite database detected.
KernelSU database not found.
KernelSU uses a SQLite database to store allowed package UIDs and policy rules. Found at: %1$s.
No KernelSU database was found at known locations.
The database is removed when KernelSU is fully uninstalled.
KernelSU Mount Points Detected
KernelSU Mount Points
KernelSU-related mount points found.
No KernelSU mount points found.
Could not read mount information.
KernelSU uses OverlayFS to overlay module files. Keywords found in /proc/mounts: %1$s.
No KernelSU-specific mount entries found.
Failed to read /proc/mounts: %1$s
Mount points are managed by KernelSU. Removed upon uninstallation.
Ensure /proc/mounts is accessible.
KernelSU Processes Running
KernelSU Processes
KernelSU daemon processes are running.
No KernelSU daemon processes found.
Could not enumerate processes.
Found processes: %1$s. ksud is the KernelSU userspace daemon that handles su requests.
No KernelSU process names found in running process list.
Process enumeration failed: %1$s
Processes stop after KernelSU is removed and device rebooted.
Ensure /proc is accessible.
KernelSU Kernel Interface
SELinux Permissive Mode
KernelSU kernel interface detected.
No KernelSU kernel interface found.
SELinux is in permissive mode (potential KernelSU indicator).
KernelSU exposes a kernel interface at /sys/kernel/ksu or /proc/sys/kernel/ksu. This confirms KernelSU is compiled into the running kernel. Found: %1$s.
No KernelSU-specific kernel interfaces were detected.
SELinux enforce status is 0 (permissive). KernelSU and other root solutions often set SELinux to permissive mode to allow unrestricted root access. This significantly reduces device security.
Flash a stock kernel without KernelSU to remove this interface.
Set SELinux to enforcing mode: `setenforce 1`. This setting persists only until reboot unless KernelSU/Magisk policies override it.
KernelSU System Properties
KernelSU-specific system properties found.
No KernelSU system properties found.
The following KernelSU properties were found: %1$s. These properties are set by KernelSU and confirm its presence.
No KernelSU-specific system properties were detected.
These properties are removed when KernelSU is uninstalled.
APatch Detected
APatch
APatch root framework detected.
APatch not detected.
APatch is a kernel-based root solution similar to KernelSU. Evidence found: %1$s. APatch patches the kernel boot image to enable root access.
No APatch files or packages were found.
Use APatch Manager to uninstall, then flash a stock boot image.
SU Binary Detected
SU Binary
su binary found at system locations.
No su binary found at common system paths.
The su (superuser) binary was found at: %1$s. The su binary allows apps to request elevated root privileges. Its presence in system paths indicates the device has been rooted.
The su binary was not found at standard system paths.
Remove the su binary by booting into recovery and deleting it, or restore via stock system image flash.
Root Management Apps Found
Root Management Apps
Root management or exploit apps detected.
No root management apps found.
Found: %1$s. These apps provide root access management, exploit capabilities, or root-related functionality that indicates a rooted device.
No known root management or exploit app packages were found.
Uninstall these apps via Settings → Apps or using adb uninstall.
Test-Keys Build Detected
Debug Build Detected
Build Signature
Device is running a test-keys signed build.
Device is running a debug build.
Build appears to use official release keys.
Build tags: \'%1$s\', Fingerprint: \'%2$s\'. Test-keys builds are signed with unofficial keys, indicating a custom or rooted ROM. Official Google-signed builds use \'release-keys\'.
Build tags: \'%1$s\'. Debug builds have additional debugging capabilities enabled and may have relaxed security restrictions.
Build tags \'%1$s\' indicate official release key signing.
Flash an official OEM ROM signed with release-keys via fastboot or OTA.
Use a release/production build for better security.
Busybox Found
Busybox
Busybox binary detected.
No Busybox binary found.
Busybox was found at: %1$s. Busybox provides Unix utilities typically not found on Android. While not root itself, it is commonly installed alongside root frameworks.
Busybox was not found at common installation paths.
Busybox is removed when the associated root framework is uninstalled.
Dangerous / Piracy Apps Detected
Dangerous / Piracy Apps
Potentially dangerous, piracy, or game-cheat apps found.
No dangerous or piracy apps detected.

Found %1$d app(s): %2$s. These packages include black-market stores, in-app purchase bypass tools, game cheats, and spyware-like apps. Their presence indicates system compromise or intentional security policy violations.
None of the known dangerous/piracy/cheat packages were found.
Uninstall these apps immediately via Settings → Apps or adb uninstall.
Extended Root-Cloaking Apps Detected
Extended Root-Cloaking Apps
Additional root-hiding or code-injection framework apps detected.
No additional root-cloaking apps detected.
Found: %1$s. Cydia Substrate is a runtime code injection framework frequently used to hide root. HideMyRoot and HideRoot variants conceal root indicators from apps.
Cydia Substrate and HideMyRoot/HideRoot variants were not found.
Uninstall root-cloaking apps via Settings → Apps.
SU Binary via PATH Enumeration Detected
SU Binary via PATH Enumeration
su binary found via dynamic PATH enumeration.
No su binary found via PATH enumeration.

su binary found at: %1$s. This check dynamically reads the PATH environment variable (like rootbeerFresh) in addition to well-known static paths, catching su binaries placed in non-standard directories that are still on the executable search path.
su was not found in any static or PATH-derived directory.
Remove the su binary and restore a stock system image.
System Paths Mounted Read-Write
System Paths Mount Check
Critical system paths mounted rw: %1$s.
No critical system paths mounted read-write.
Could not run mount command.

The following paths that should be read-only are mounted read-write: %1$s. This is detected by parsing the \`mount\` command output with SDK-version-aware field offsets (rootbeerFresh technique). A rw-mounted /system indicates the system partition has been modified.
mount command output shows all sensitive paths are read-only.
The mount command returned no output.
Remount system partition as read-only: \`adb shell mount -o remount,ro /system\`.
Ensure /proc is accessible.
AVB Boot State
AVB Boot State: YELLOW
AVB Boot State: ORANGE (Unlocked)
AVB Boot State: RED (Integrity Failure)
AVB Boot State: Unknown
Verified Boot state is GREEN.
Verified Boot state is YELLOW (custom ROM / self-signed).
Verified Boot state is ORANGE -- bootloader is UNLOCKED.
Verified Boot state is RED -- boot image verification FAILED.
Unexpected Verified Boot state: %1$s.
AVB state property not available.
Could not determine AVB boot state.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s. The device booted from verified, unmodified system partitions. This is the strongest boot integrity assurance.

ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s. The bootloader verified the boot image against a user-installed key rather than a manufacturer key. This indicates a custom ROM or user-enrolled AVB key. The device may be running unofficial firmware.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s. The bootloader is unlocked and no signature is enforced. The device can boot arbitrary unsigned images. This is a strong indicator of a rooted or modified device.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s. The device failed to verify the integrity of the boot image. This may indicate a modified bootloader or corrupted system partition.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate=%1$s is not a standard value. Expected: green, yellow, orange, or red.
ro.boot.verifiedbootstate is empty or inaccessible. This is normal on older devices (pre-Android 8) that do not support AVB 2.0.
Exception reading AVB properties: %1$s
Flash official OEM firmware to restore GREEN verified boot state.
Re-lock the bootloader via \'fastboot flashing lock\' after restoring stock firmware.
Restore factory firmware via fastboot or OTA.
Check device firmware integrity.
TEE Key Generation: Software Only
TEE Key Generation
AES key was generated in software -- no hardware TEE backing.
AES key was generated inside secure hardware (%1$s).
TEE key generation check could not be completed.

A probe AES key was generated via AndroidKeyStore but KeyInfo reports it was created in the software emulation layer. This strongly suggests the device is an emulator, or the TEE has been bypassed (e.g. by a Keymaster/KeyMint shim module). Sensitive cryptographic material is NOT protected by secure hardware.

A probe AES key was generated via AndroidKeyStore and KeyInfo confirms it resides in %1$s. This check cannot be spoofed by Magisk property hiding because it exercises the actual TEE subsystem via the Binder-based Keymaster/KeyMint HAL.
Exception during probe key generation: %1$s
Use a real device with a hardware TEE for secure key operations.
Hardware-Backed RSA Key: Software Only
Hardware-Backed RSA Key
RSA key pair is in software keystore, not secure hardware.
RSA-2048 key pair resides in secure hardware (TEE/StrongBox).
Keystore attestation check could not be completed.

The AndroidKeyStore RSA key was generated in the software emulation layer rather than in a hardware TEE. This may indicate an emulator, a rooted device with Magisk hiding active, or a device that does not have a hardware-backed keystore.
RSA-2048 key generated via AndroidKeyStore resides inside the Trusted Execution Environment or StrongBox HSM. Private key material never leaves secure hardware.
Exception during RSA key generation/inspection: %1$s
Use a device with hardware-backed key storage for sensitive operations.
Root Daemon Processes Running
Root Daemon Processes
Active root framework daemon(s) detected in process list.
No known root framework daemons found in process list.
Could not scan process list.

The following root-related processes are running: %1$s. lspd is the LSPosed/Xposed framework daemon. ksud is the KernelSU daemon. magiskd is the Magisk daemon. zygiskd is the Zygisk companion daemon. These confirm an active root framework even if file artifacts are hidden.
Scanned /proc for lspd, ksud, magiskd, zygiskd, riru, apd, and other known root framework daemons. None were found.
Exception reading /proc: %1$s
Uninstall the root framework and reboot to stop these daemons.
System Property Tampering
System Property Tampering
System properties show signs of modification.
No system property tampering detected.

The following property inconsistencies were detected: %1$s. Magisk resetprop can modify any system property after boot, but Android framework constants baked into the app JVM at launch cannot be changed retroactively -- a mismatch proves tampering.
Build.TAGS vs live ro.build.tags, security patch cross-check, fingerprint vs device name, and Magisk prop checks all passed. System properties are consistent with the stock build.
Restore stock boot image to reset system properties to factory values.
Su Binary (Extended Scan)
Su Binary (Extended Scan)
Su binary or root management files found.
No su binary or root management files found.
The following su-related paths were found: %1$s. Presence of a su binary is a strong indicator that the device is rooted.
Scanned %1$d known su binary paths and checked PATH. No su binary was found accessible on this device.
Remove all root frameworks (Magisk, KernelSU, APatch, SuperSU) and restore the stock boot image.
Magisk Files Detected
Magisk Files
Magisk-specific files or directories found on the filesystem.
No Magisk-specific files detected.
The following Magisk-specific files were found: %1$s. These files are created by Magisk during installation and runtime. Their presence strongly indicates that Magisk is installed on this device.
None of the known Magisk file paths were accessible or present.
To remove Magisk: Open Magisk Manager → Uninstall → Complete Uninstall. Or flash the stock boot.img via fastboot: `fastboot flash boot stock_boot.img`.
Magisk Directories Detected
Magisk Directories
Magisk working directories found.
No Magisk working directories found.
Directories %1$s exist. Magisk uses /data/adb/magisk as its primary working directory and /data/adb/modules to store installed modules. These are created during Magisk installation.
Magisk working directories were not found.
Uninstall Magisk via Magisk Manager or reflash stock boot image.
Magisk Database Found
Magisk Database
Magisk SQLite database detected.
Magisk database not found.
The Magisk database (magisk.db) stores policy rules, module configurations, and superuser grant records. Found at: %1$s.
No Magisk SQLite database was found at known locations.
The database will be removed when Magisk is fully uninstalled.
Magisk App Detected
Magisk App Packages
Magisk Manager or related apps installed.
No Magisk Manager packages found.
The following Magisk-related packages were found: %1$s. These apps are the primary management interface for Magisk. Their presence confirms Magisk installation.
No known Magisk Manager package names were found installed.
Uninstall via Magisk Manager first (to properly remove root), then uninstall the Magisk Manager app itself.
Magisk SU Binary Found
Magisk SU Binary
Magisk su or core binaries detected.
No Magisk su or core binaries found.
Magisk installs its su binary and core components to manage root access. Found: %1$s. These binaries handle root permission requests and policy enforcement.
Magisk core binaries were not found at known paths.
These binaries are removed when Magisk is uninstalled via Magisk Manager → Uninstall.
Magisk Unix Socket Detected
Magisk Unix Socket
Magisk daemon socket found in /proc/net/unix.
No Magisk daemon socket found.
Magisk runs a background daemon that listens on a Unix socket. The socket name \'%1$s\' was found in /proc/net/unix, indicating the Magisk daemon is currently running.
No Magisk-related socket names were found in /proc/net/unix.
Could not read /proc/net/unix: %1$s
The socket disappears when Magisk is uninstalled and the device is rebooted.
Magisk Mount Points Detected
Magisk Mount Points
Magisk-related mount points found in /proc/mounts.
No Magisk mount points found.
Magisk uses bind mounts to overlay modified files onto the system without actually modifying the underlying partition. Keywords found: %1$s.
No Magisk-specific mount entries found in /proc/mounts.
Could not read /proc/mounts: %1$s
Mount points are managed by Magisk daemon. They are removed upon uninstallation.
Suspicious System Props
Magisk System Properties
Suspicious or Magisk-related system properties found.
No suspicious Magisk-related system properties found.
The following properties suggest root access or Magisk presence: %1$s. ro.debuggable=1 and ro.secure=0 are typical on rooted/debuggable builds. ro.magisk.version is set by Magisk directly.
System properties appear to be in their stock configuration.
These properties are set by Magisk or during device rooting. Restore the stock boot image to reset these properties.
Zygisk Library Detected
Zygisk Library
Zygisk (Magisk in Zygote) library files found.
No Zygisk library files found.

Zygisk is Magisk\'s feature that runs in the Zygote process, enabling code injection into every app. Found: %1$s. This allows bypassing many detection methods and enables powerful module functionality.
Zygisk library files were not found. Either Zygisk is disabled or Magisk is not installed.
Disable Zygisk in Magisk settings or fully uninstall Magisk.
MagiskHide / Modified Fingerprint
MagiskHide / Fingerprint
MagiskHide artifacts or modified build fingerprint detected.
No MagiskHide artifacts or fingerprint modifications detected.
Evidence: %1$s. MagiskHide (or its successor DenyList) attempts to hide Magisk from specific apps. A modified build fingerprint may indicate SafetyNet bypass attempts.
No MagiskHide files found and build fingerprint appears unmodified.
Disable MagiskHide/DenyList in Magisk settings. Use a stock, unmodified build fingerprint. Consider using a Play Integrity fix module carefully.
Magisk Processes Running
Magisk Processes
Magisk-related processes are currently running.
No Magisk processes found running.
No Magisk processes found.
The following Magisk processes were found running: %1$s. magiskd is the Magisk daemon that manages root requests. These processes confirm Magisk is active on the device.
No Magisk-related process names were found in /proc.
Process enumeration could not be completed: %1$s
Processes will stop after Magisk is uninstalled and device rebooted.
Hidden Magisk Manager Detected
Hidden Magisk Manager
An app disguised as Magisk Manager was found.
No hidden Magisk Manager detected.
Could not scan for hidden Magisk Manager.
Magisk Manager can be hidden under a random package name to evade detection. Found potential hidden manager: %1$s.
No apps with Magisk Manager characteristics were found under non-standard package names.
Package scan could not be completed: %1$s
Open the app and uninstall Magisk, or use adb to remove the package.
Magisk /dev Files Detected
Magisk /dev Files
Magisk-specific files or sockets found in /dev.
No Magisk /dev files or sockets found.
Found: %1$s. Magisk uses the /dev filesystem (tmpfs) to create control sockets and mirror points that it can access from any SELinux context. These files only exist on devices with active Magisk.
No Magisk-specific files were found under /dev.
Uninstall Magisk to remove these /dev artifacts.
Zygisk Library Injected
Zygisk Library
Zygisk native library is loaded in this process.
No Zygisk library found in process memory maps.
No Zygisk library found in process memory.

Zygisk library found in /proc/self/maps: %1$s. Zygisk (Zygote + Magisk) injects its native library into every app process by hooking the Zygote process before forks. This confirms Zygisk is actively running.
No Zygisk native library was found in /proc/self/maps.
Could not read /proc/self/maps: %1$s
Disable Zygisk in Magisk settings or uninstall Magisk.
Magisk Stub App Detected
Magisk Stub App
App with Magisk stub APK characteristics found.
No Magisk stub app detected.
Package(s) with Magisk stub metadata: %1$s. Magisk\'s hide feature repackages itself as an innocent-looking app. The stub retains Magisk\'s content provider authority and specific permission set.
No installed packages match the Magisk stub APK signature.
Open the stub app and uninstall Magisk through it.
Magisk SELinux Context Detected
Magisk SELinux Context
Magisk-related SELinux context found.
No Magisk SELinux contexts detected.
No Magisk SELinux context found.

Found: %1$s. Magisk injects custom SELinux policy rules and some versions run under their own SELinux context (u:r:magisk:s0). A non-standard SELinux context for a root-related process confirms Magisk activity.
No Magisk-related SELinux contexts found in /proc/*/attr/current.
Could not read /proc/self/attr/current: %1$s
Remove Magisk to restore standard SELinux policy.
Magisk APEX Overlay Detected
Magisk APEX Overlay
Magisk overlay inside /apex detected.
No Magisk APEX overlay detected.

Found: %1$s. Magisk (v24+) can inject modules into APEX (Android Pony EXpress) partition libraries by bind-mounting over them. The \'orig\' directory is created as a backup of the original APEX content. These artefacts are only present when Magisk modules are active.
No Magisk-specific files were found inside /apex.
Disable APEX-targeting modules in Magisk Manager or uninstall Magisk.
Magisk File Descriptors Detected
Magisk File Descriptors
This process has open file descriptors pointing to Magisk paths.
No Magisk-related file descriptors found in /proc/self/fd.
Found in /proc/self/fd: %1$s. Magisk keeps open FDs to its mirror mount points and socket files. The presence of these FDs in an app process confirms Magisk has touched this process.
No open file descriptors point to Magisk paths.
Remove Magisk to clear these file descriptor references.
Native Bridge Injection Detected
Native Bridge
Riru/Zygisk native bridge injection detected.
No suspicious native bridge injection found.

ro.dalvik.vm.native.bridge = \'%1$s\'. Riru (a predecessor of Zygisk) hijacks the ART native bridge mechanism by replacing this property with its own loader library. A suspicious value here is a strong indicator of Riru/Zygisk-based injection.
ro.dalvik.vm.native.bridge is \'%1$s\' \u2014 stock value.
Remove the Riru or Zygisk Magisk module to restore the normal native bridge.
Zygisk Companion Daemon Detected
Zygisk/Magisk Daemon Processes
Zygisk/Magisk daemon processes are running.
No Zygisk/Magisk daemon processes found.

Found daemon process(es): %1$s. Zygisk Next (a standalone Zygisk implementation) runs privileged daemon processes named zn-daemon, zn-nsdaemon, and zn-zygisk-companion. Magisk itself runs as \'magiskd\'. These are definitive indicators of active root injection.
No Zygisk Next or Magisk daemon process names were found in /proc/*/cmdline.
Uninstall Magisk or the Zygisk Next module to remove these daemon processes.
Magisk Hook Latency Anomaly
File Access Timing
Suspicious file access latency suggests Magisk DenyList hooks.
No suspicious timing anomaly for Magisk file access.
Timing check inconclusive.

File.exists(\'%1$s\') took %2$sms (%3$s\u00D7 slower than baseline). Magisk\'s DenyList patches intercept filesystem calls to hide Magisk files. This interception adds measurable latency compared to accessing non-hooked paths.
File.exists() latency is within normal range (ratio=%1$s\u00D7, %2$sms). No Magisk DenyList hook latency detected.
Could not complete timing check: %1$s
Disable Magisk DenyList or uninstall Magisk.
Root/Hook Libraries in Process Memory
Root/Hook Libraries
Root or hook framework native libraries are loaded in this process.
No root/hook framework native libraries found in process memory.

Found in /proc/self/maps: %1$s. These are native libraries belonging to Magisk (libsu), Riru, and inline hook engines (Dobby, SandHook, Pine, Whale) used by Xposed-compatible frameworks. Their presence confirms active injection into this process.
No known root/hook library names were found in /proc/self/maps.
Remove the relevant root framework or module to prevent library injection.
Xposed/LSPosed Manager Detected
Xposed Manager Packages
Xposed framework management app installed.
No Xposed/LSPosed manager packages found.
Found: %1$s. These apps manage Xposed/LSPosed/EdXposed frameworks. Xposed frameworks hook into any Android app or system service at runtime.
No known Xposed framework manager packages are installed.
Uninstall via the framework manager. Flash a stock boot image to fully remove hooks from Zygote.
Xposed Framework Files Found
Xposed Framework Files
Original Xposed framework files detected.
No original Xposed framework files found.
Found: %1$s. XposedBridge.jar is the core Xposed hooking library. app_process_xposed is the modified app_process binary used to bootstrap Xposed at Zygote startup.
No known Xposed file paths were found on this device.
Use Xposed Installer to uninstall, or manually restore the stock app_process binary.
LSPosed Module Files Found
LSPosed Files
LSPosed framework files detected.
No LSPosed files found.
Found: %1$s. LSPosed is the modern Xposed implementation for Android 8+. It runs as a Zygisk or Riru module and enables per-app Xposed module scoping.
No LSPosed-specific file paths were found.
Remove via LSPosed Manager \u2192 Uninstall, or remove the Magisk/KernelSU module.
EdXposed Files Found
EdXposed Files
EdXposed framework files detected.
No EdXposed files found.
Found: %1$s. EdXposed is an Xposed implementation for Android 8\u201311 running as a Riru module. It enables system-wide code hooks compatible with legacy Xposed modules.
No EdXposed-specific file paths were found.
Remove via EdXposed Manager or delete the Riru/Magisk module.
XposedBridge Class Loaded
XposedBridge Class
XposedBridge class is present in the classpath.
XposedBridge class not found in classpath.

The class \'de.robv.android.xposed.XposedBridge\' was successfully loaded. This class is the core of the Xposed framework and is only present when Xposed (or a compatible fork like LSPosed/EdXposed) is active in this process.
XposedBridge class could not be loaded \u2014 Xposed is not active in this process.
Class check did not locate XposedBridge: %1$s
Uninstall the Xposed/LSPosed/EdXposed framework.
Xposed Hooks in Stack Trace
Xposed in Stack Trace
Xposed framework detected in exception call stack.
No Xposed framework entries in exception stack trace.
Xposed/LSPlant framework entries were found in the exception stack trace. This confirms that Xposed is actively hooking method calls in this process.
Exception stack trace shows no Xposed framework methods.
Uninstall Xposed/LSPosed framework.
Xposed System Properties Found
Xposed System Properties
Xposed-specific system properties found.
No Xposed system properties found.
Found: %1$s. These properties are set by the Xposed framework or its installer.
No Xposed-specific system properties were detected.
Properties are removed when Xposed is properly uninstalled.
Virtual App Framework Detected
Virtual App Frameworks
Virtual app / multi-account framework detected.
No virtual app frameworks detected.

Found: %1$s. Virtual frameworks like VirtualApp and Parallel Space create virtualized Android environments. They can run apps in sandboxed clones and may bypass detection by running apps inside a modified container.
No known virtual framework packages were found.
Uninstall virtual framework apps via Settings \u2192 Apps.
LSPosed Daemon Socket Detected
LSPosed Daemon Socket
LSPosed daemon Unix socket is active.
No LSPosed daemon socket found.
Found socket(s) in /proc/net/unix: %1$s. LSPosed (lspd) uses a Unix domain socket for IPC between its daemon and the manager app. The presence of this socket confirms lspd is currently running.
No lspd socket names found in /proc/net/unix.
Could not read /proc/net/unix: %1$s
Remove LSPosed via the LSPosed Manager app or delete the Magisk/KSU module.
LSPosed Files in Process Memory
LSPosed Files in Process
LSPosed/LSPlant files are loaded into this process.
No LSPosed/LSPlant files found in process memory.

Found in /proc/self/maps: %1$s. LSPosed injects its hook engine (LSPlant) and module DEX files into every scoped app process. These mapped paths are a definitive indicator of active LSPosed injection.
No LSPosed-related files were found in /proc/self/maps.
Could not read /proc/self/maps: %1$s
Remove LSPosed or disable module scoping for this app.
LSPosed Configuration Files Found
LSPosed Configuration Files
LSPosed configuration or module files detected.
No LSPosed configuration files found.
Found: %1$s. LSPosed stores its daemon configuration and module scope lists in /data/misc/lspd/. The presence of these files confirms LSPosed is or was installed.
No LSPosed config paths were found on disk.
Uninstall LSPosed via the manager app or remove the Magisk/KSU module directory.
Hooked Java Methods Detected
Java Method Hooks
Java methods are hooked by LSPosed/LSPlant.
No Java method hooks detected via reflection.
Hooked methods detected: %1$s. LSPosed uses the LSPlant library as its ART hook engine. Hooked methods have their ArtMethod implementation pointer replaced, which is detectable via reflection.
No LSPlant/XposedBridge hook indicators found in reflection or stack traces.
Remove LSPosed or disable modules that hook these methods.
LSPosed Module Targeting This App
LSPosed Module Scope
An LSPosed module is scoped to hook this application.
No LSPosed module appears to be scoped to this app.

Found \'%1$s\' in LSPosed scope file(s): %2$s. LSPosed records which apps each module should be injected into. Seeing this app\'s package name in the scope config confirms an active module is targeting it.
This app\'s package name was not found in any readable LSPosed scope config.
Open LSPosed Manager and disable all modules scoped to this app.
LSPosed Internal Classes Detected
LSPosed Internal Classes
LSPosed internal classes are loaded in this process.
No LSPosed internal classes found in classpath.

Successfully loaded: %1$s. These are LSPosed\'s private implementation classes (lspd core). They are only present in the classpath when LSPosed has injected into this process. This is the most reliable Java-layer indicator of active LSPosed hooking.
None of the known LSPosed lspd implementation classes could be loaded.
Uninstall LSPosed or disable module scoping for this application.
XposedBridge Version Properties Detected
XposedBridge Version Properties
Xposed framework version properties or bridge fields found.
No XposedBridge version properties found.

Found: %1$s. The Xposed framework sets \'xposed.bridge.version\' as a system property and exposes static fields on XposedBridge. These are definitive indicators that LSPosed (or another Xposed fork) is active.
No Xposed version system properties or bridge fields detected.
Uninstall LSPosed/Xposed to remove these properties.
LSPosed ClassLoader in Chain
ClassLoader Chain
LSPosed/Xposed ClassLoader found in the ClassLoader hierarchy.
No LSPosed/Xposed ClassLoader found in the ClassLoader hierarchy.

Found: %1$s. LSPosed wraps the app\'s ClassLoader with its own loader to inject Xposed modules. A ClassLoader with \'lsposed\', \'lspd\', or \'edxposed\' in its class name is a direct indicator of active injection.
The ClassLoader parent chain contains no known hook framework class names.
Remove LSPosed or disable modules for this app.
In-Memory DEX ClassLoader Detected
In-Memory DEX ClassLoader
Anonymous/in-memory DEX modules detected in ClassLoader.
No in-memory DEX entries found in ClassLoader hierarchy.

Found: %1$s. LSPosed loads Xposed module APK/DEX files directly into memory using InMemoryDexClassLoader. In-memory DEX elements have no file path \u2014 this is one of the most reliable Java-layer indicators of active LSPosed module injection.
All DEX entries in the ClassLoader hierarchy reference real files on disk.
Disable all LSPosed modules for this app via LSPosed Manager.
LSPosed Data Directory Exists
LSPosed Data Directories
LSPosed/Xposed manager app data directory found.
No LSPosed/Xposed manager data directories found.
Found: %1$s. Even when Magisk DenyList hides the LSPosed Manager APK from PackageManager, its app data directory remains on disk. Its existence proves LSPosed was or is installed on this device.
No known LSPosed/Xposed data directories were found in /data/user/0/ or /data/data/.
Uninstall LSPosed Manager via the app itself or via adb.
Zygisk Environment/Properties Detected
Zygisk Environment/Properties
Zygisk-specific environment variables or system properties found.
No Zygisk environment variables or properties found.
Found: %1$s. ZYGISK_ENABLED is set by Magisk when Zygisk is active. These variables/properties are not present on stock devices and confirm Zygisk is running.
ZYGISK_ENABLED is not set; no Zygisk system properties detected.
Disable Zygisk in Magisk settings or uninstall Magisk/Zygisk Next.
LSPosed/lspd Frames in Stack Trace
LSPosed/lspd Stack Trace
LSPosed/lspd class names detected in exception stack trace.
No LSPosed/lspd class names found in stack trace.
Found frames: %1$s. When LSPosed is injected and a module is scoped to this app, lspd class names appear in the call stack.
No lsposed/lspd/edxposed class names appeared in the exception stack trace.
Remove LSPosed or disable all modules scoped to this app.
Inline Hook Detected via SMAPS
SMAPS Inline Hook Check
Critical library code segments have dirty (modified) pages.
No inline hook signatures found in SMAPS.

Affected libraries: %1$s. A non-zero Private_Dirty value in an r-xp (read-execute) code segment means that code pages were copied due to Linux Copy-on-Write. This is the fingerprint of inline hooking by LSPosed (via LSPlant), Frida, or a Zygisk module.
All critical library code segments have Private_Dirty = 0.
Remove LSPosed, Frida gadget, or any Zygisk hooking modules.
Zygisk Module Injected into Process
Zygisk Module Injection
A Zygisk module library is mapped into this process.
No Zygisk module libraries found in process memory map.

Found Zygisk module path(s) in /proc/self/maps: %1$s. Zygisk loads each enabled module\'s native library directly into the app process from /data/adb/modules/<name>/zygisk/. Seeing this path confirms that Zygisk is active and has injected a module here.
No /data/adb/modules/*/zygisk/ paths found in /proc/self/maps.
Disable Zygisk in Magisk settings or remove injecting modules.
LSPosed Daemon Process Detected
LSPosed Daemon Process
The LSPosed daemon (lspd) is running on this device.
No LSPosed daemon (lspd) process found.
A process named \'lspd\' was found in /proc. lspd is the background daemon of the LSPosed framework that manages hook modules and injects code into app processes via Zygote. Found: %1$s.
No process named \'lspd\' was found in /proc.
Uninstall LSPosed via its manager app and reboot to stop the daemon.
Su Binary Found
Su Binary Check
A su binary was found on the device.
No su binary found in standard locations.
Found at: %1$s
No su binary found in the checked locations.
Remove the su binary from the reported path.
System Partition Mounted Read-Write
One or more system partitions are mounted read-write.
System partitions are mounted read-only.
Could not check system mount flags.
Read-write mount points: %1$s. System partitions should be mounted read-only on secure devices.
All checked system partitions are mounted read-only.
Remount system partitions as read-only or restore factory image.
Verify mount flags manually via adb shell mount.
Bootloader Unlocked
Bootloader Lock Status
The bootloader appears to be unlocked.
No bootloader unlock indicators found.
Indicators: %1$s
No bootloader unlock indicators were detected.
Re-lock the bootloader via OEM/fastboot if possible.
dm-verity Disabled
dm-verity Status
dm-verity is disabled, allowing system partition modification.
dm-verity appears to be enabled.
Indicators: %1$s. dm-verity verifies system partition integrity on boot.
No dm-verity disable indicators detected.
Re-enable dm-verity via fastboot or restore the factory image.
Dangerous System Properties Set
Dangerous System Properties
Dangerous system properties associated with root or debugging are set.
No dangerous system properties detected.
Found: %1$s
No dangerous system properties were found.
Remove or unset the dangerous properties and reboot.
Developer Options Enabled
Android Developer Options are enabled on this device.
Developer Options are disabled.
Could not determine Developer Options status.
The Developer Options setting is enabled (value=1).
Developer Options setting is disabled (value=0).
Disable via Settings → System → Developer Options → toggle off.
Check manually in Settings → System → Developer Options.
ADB Debugging Enabled
USB debugging (ADB) is enabled on this device.
ADB debugging is disabled.
Could not determine ADB status.
The adb_enabled setting is enabled (value=1).
ADB debugging is not enabled (value=0).
Disable via Settings → Developer Options → USB Debugging → toggle off.
Check manually in Settings → Developer Options.
Frida Server Process Detected
A Frida server process is running on this device.
No Frida server process detected.
Could not scan processes for Frida.
Frida processes found: %1$s
No Frida server processes found in /proc.
Kill and uninstall frida-server from the device.
Verify manually using adb shell ps.
Frida Default Port Open
Frida Default Port Check
Frida\'s default port (27042) is open on this device.
Frida\'s default port is not open.
Port 27042 is open — this is the default Frida server port.
Port 27042 is not open.
Kill frida-server and remove it from the device.
Frida Files Found
Frida Files Check
Frida-related files were found on the filesystem.
No Frida-related files found.
Found: %1$s
No Frida files found in the checked locations.
Delete the Frida files and remove frida-server from the device.
Riru (Riru Core) Detected
Riru Module Check
Riru (Riru Core) injection framework is present.
No Riru framework files detected.
Found Riru indicators: %1$s
No Riru-related files or processes were found.
Remove the Riru module via Magisk Manager and reboot.
Custom Recovery Detected
Custom Recovery Check
A custom recovery (TWRP or similar) is installed.
No custom recovery indicators found.
Custom recovery indicators: %1$s
No custom recovery indicators were detected.
Flash the stock recovery and re-lock the bootloader.
Emulator Detected
Emulator Check
This device appears to be running in an emulator.
No emulator indicators detected.
Emulator indicators: %1$s
No emulator-related properties or files were found.
Run the app on a physical device instead of an emulator.
Injected Libraries Detected
Suspicious libraries are mapped into this process.
No injected libraries detected in process memory.
Could not read process memory maps.
Suspicious paths: %1$s
No suspicious library paths found in /proc/self/maps.
Remove the hooking framework or injected library.
Check /proc/self/maps manually for suspicious entries.
Zygisk Companion Socket Detected
Zygisk Companion Socket
Zygisk companion sockets were found in the abstract socket namespace.
No Zygisk companion sockets found.
Could not scan abstract socket namespace.
Sockets found: %1$s. Zygisk creates abstract sockets for IPC between the companion process and injected code.
No Zygisk socket names found in /proc/net/unix.
Disable Zygisk in Magisk settings and reboot.
Check /proc/net/unix manually for Zygisk socket entries.
Magisk Modules Detected
Active Magisk modules were found on this device.
No active Magisk modules found.
The Magisk modules directory does not exist.
Active modules: %1$s
No enabled module directories found in /data/adb/modules/.
The /data/adb/modules/ directory does not exist — Magisk is likely not installed.
Remove or disable Magisk modules via Magisk Manager.
Debugger Attached
Debugger Attach Check
A debugger is attached to this process.
No debugger is attached to this process.
Could not read process status to check for debugger.
TracerPid=%1$d — a non-zero TracerPid in /proc/self/status indicates a debugger is attached.
TracerPid=0 — no debugger attached.
Detach the debugger and do not run the app under a debugger.
Check /proc/self/status TracerPid manually.
Root Cloaking Detected
Root Cloaking Check
Root-cloaking software (MagiskHide, Shamiko, etc.) is active.
No root cloaking indicators detected.
Cloaking indicators: %1$s
No root cloaking indicators were found.
Disable MagiskHide/DenyList or remove cloaking modules and reboot.
Root Kernel Modules Loaded
Suspicious kernel modules associated with root are loaded.
No suspicious kernel modules found.
Could not read kernel module list.
Suspicious modules: %1$s
No suspicious kernel modules found in /proc/modules.
Remove or unload the suspicious kernel modules and reboot.
Check /proc/modules manually for suspicious entries.
No action required.
System Partition Mount
Developer Options
USB Debugging
Frida Process Check
Injected Libraries Check
Root Kernel Modules Check
Magisk Modules Check
LD_PRELOAD / LD_LIBRARY_PATH Set
Linker environment variable injection detected.

Found: %1$s. Setting LD_PRELOAD causes the dynamic linker to load an attacker-chosen library before all others, enabling function interception in any process. This is used by Frida gadget, Substrate-style hooks, and some su wrappers.
Remove any LD_PRELOAD/LD_LIBRARY_PATH from the environment. Uninstall any hooking framework that injects via the linker.
LD_PRELOAD Environment
No LD_PRELOAD injection detected.
LD_PRELOAD and LD_LIBRARY_PATH are not set to suspicious values.
Could not read process environment.
Ensure /proc/self/environ is accessible.
Elevated Linux Capabilities
Process has non-zero Linux capabilities.

CapPrm=%1$s, CapEff=%2$s. Normal Android apps run with zero capabilities. Non-zero values indicate this process was granted elevated Linux privileges, which can happen when su or a root framework manipulates the process.
Revoke elevated capabilities. These are normally granted by root frameworks.
Linux Capabilities
Process has no elevated Linux capabilities.
CapPrm=0 and CapEff=0 — normal for a sandboxed Android app.
Could not read process status.
Ensure /proc/self/status is accessible.
Magisk/KSU Overlay Mounts Detected
Root framework overlay mount entries found in /proc/self/mountinfo.

/proc/self/mountinfo shows %1$d mount entry/entries sourced from Magisk/KSU directories (/data/adb/magisk or /data/adb/ksu). These are bind-mounts created by the root framework\'s module system to overlay modified files over the original system partition.
Remove root framework modules or uninstall the root framework entirely.
Root Overlay Mounts
No Magisk/KSU overlay mounts found.
No root framework markers were found in /proc/self/mountinfo.
Could not read /proc/self/mountinfo.
Ensure /proc/self/mountinfo is accessible.
tmpfs Mounted at /sbin
tmpfs is mounted over /sbin — legacy Magisk indicator.
Magisk versions prior to v24 mounted a tmpfs over /sbin to store its binaries and hide them from detection. A tmpfs on /sbin on a production device is a strong Magisk indicator.
This is removed when Magisk is uninstalled.
tmpfs at /sbin
No tmpfs mount at /sbin.
/sbin does not have a tmpfs mount.
Could not read /proc/mounts.
Ensure /proc/mounts is accessible.
Unexpected Root Processes
Unexpected processes running as uid=0 (root).
Found %1$d unexpected root process(es): %2$s. On a stock device, the set of uid=0 processes is fixed. Extra root processes indicate active root frameworks (magiskd, ksud, etc.).
Stop or remove the root framework to eliminate unexpected root processes.
Privileged Processes
No unexpected uid=0 processes found.
Only expected system processes were found running as root.
Could not enumerate processes.
Ensure /proc is accessible.
Terminal / Hacking Apps Detected
Terminal emulator or security tool apps installed.
Found %1$d app(s): %2$s. These apps provide terminal access, SSH, scripting environments, or advanced file system access. Many require or facilitate root access.
Uninstall terminal and hacking apps if not needed for legitimate use.
Terminal / Hacking Apps
No terminal emulator or hacking apps detected.
No known terminal or security tool packages were found.
Security Patch Very Outdated
Security patch is %1$d months old (>= 2 years).

Security patch level: %1$s (%2$d months ago). Rooted devices often avoid OTA updates to preserve root access, resulting in severely outdated security patches. An extremely old patch level exposes the device to known CVEs.
Apply all available OTA/security updates.
Security Patch Outdated
Security patch is %1$d months old (>= 1 year).
Security patch level: %1$s (%2$d months ago). Keeping the patch level current is important for device security.
Apply all available security updates from the manufacturer.
Security Patch Level
Security patch level is reasonably current (< 12 months).
Patch date: %1$s (%2$d months ago).
Could not determine security patch age.
Check Settings → About Phone → Android security patch level.
Test-Keys Build Detected
Device is running a test-keys signed build.

ro.build.tags=%1$s. Production (retail) Android builds are signed with release keys and report \'release-keys\'. \'test-keys\' means the build was signed with Android\'s publicly known AOSP test keys, which is typical for custom ROMs (LineageOS, AOSP builds) and developer images. Such builds often have root access or weaker security policies.
Use an OEM-provided, release-keys signed official ROM.
Build Signing Keys
Build is signed with release keys.
ro.build.tags=%1$s — not test-keys.
OEM Unlock Enabled
OEM unlock is currently enabled.
OEM unlock enabled: %1$s. When OEM unlock is allowed, the user can unlock the bootloader from Developer Options. An enabled OEM unlock setting indicates the device is prepared for rooting.
Disable OEM unlock via Settings → Developer Options → OEM unlocking → toggle off.
OEM Unlock
OEM unlock does not appear to be enabled.
No OEM unlock enabled indicators found.
Mock Locations Enabled
Mock location provider is enabled.
Mock locations allow any app to supply fake GPS coordinates. This is commonly used for GPS spoofing and game cheating. Detail: %1$s
Disable mock location: Settings → Developer Options → Mock location app → None.
Mock Locations
Mock locations are not enabled.
allow_mock_location=0 and no mock location app is configured.
Could not read mock location settings.
Check manually in Settings → Developer Options.
ADB over Wi-Fi Active
ADB over Wi-Fi port is listening.

Port(s) %1$s are in LISTEN state. Port 5555 is the ADB-over-Wi-Fi default. Any device on the same network can connect and issue ADB commands, including installing/removing apps and (on debug builds) gaining a shell.
Disable via \'adb disconnect\' or Settings → Wireless Debugging → toggle off.
ADB over Wi-Fi
No ADB Wi-Fi ports detected as listening.
Ports 5555/5037 are not in LISTEN state.
BusyBox Installed
BusyBox binary found.

Found: %1$s. BusyBox is a suite of Unix command-line utilities (ls, cp, wget, etc.) commonly installed alongside root to extend shell capabilities. Presence in /system/xbin or /sbin usually requires root to have been installed.
Remove via BusyBox uninstaller app or manually delete the binary.
BusyBox
BusyBox not found.
No BusyBox binary found at common locations.
SUID su Binary Detected
SUID root su binary found.
SUID binaries: %1$s. A SUID root su binary can be executed by any user to gain root privileges. This is the classic root-granting mechanism on Unix/Android.
Remove the su binary or revoke its SUID bit: \'chmod 755 /system/bin/su\'
SUID su Binary
No SUID su binary detected.
No SUID root su binary was found at common paths.
Detection-Bypass Module Found
Root detection bypass module detected.

Found: %1$s. Shamiko, Zygisk-Assistant, and ReZygisk are Zygisk modules specifically designed to hide root from apps using DenyList / allow-listing. Play Integrity Fix modules spoof the Play Integrity API verdict. These indicate active attempts to conceal root from this app.
Remove hide modules from Magisk Manager → Modules.
Detection-Bypass Modules
No Shamiko/ZygiskAssist detection-bypass modules found.
No known detection-bypass module directories or packages were found.
Suspicious System Bind-Mounts
Root-framework bind-mounts over /system detected.
Magisk and KernelSU modules replace files in /system by bind-mounting modified copies from /data/adb. Found bind-mounts: %1$s. These replace stock system files with module-provided versions.
Remove root modules or uninstall the root framework.
System Bind-Mounts
No suspicious /system bind-mounts found.
No root-framework (/data/adb) sourced bind-mounts over /system, /vendor or /product found.
Could not read /proc/self/mountinfo.
Ensure /proc/self/mountinfo is accessible.
LSPosed API Property Detected
LSPosed-specific system properties found.
Luna-method (findlsp): __system_property_get on LSPosed API property. Found: %1$s. persist.lsp.api is set by LSPosed and contains its API version number.
Disable or uninstall LSPosed to remove these properties.
LSPosed API Property
No LSPosed-specific system properties found.
Luna-method (findlsp): None of the known LSPosed system properties (persist.lsp.api, init.svc.lspd) were found.
KernelSU Daemon Service Property
KernelSU daemon service properties found.
Luna-method (findksu): __system_property_get confirmed KernelSU daemon. Found: %1$s. init.svc.ksuud is set by the Android init system when the KernelSU userspace daemon (ksuud) is active.
Uninstall KernelSU via the KernelSU Manager app or flash a stock kernel.
No KernelSU daemon service properties found.
Luna-method (findksu): None of the KernelSU service properties (init.svc.ksuud, init.svc.ksu) were found.
Magisk Daemon Service Property
Magisk daemon service property found.
Luna-method (checkappnum): __system_property_get confirmed Magisk daemon. Found: %1$s. init.svc.magiskd is set by the Android init system when the Magisk daemon is running.
Uninstall Magisk via the Magisk Manager app.
No Magisk daemon service properties found.
Luna-method (checkappnum): None of the known Magisk service properties were found.
Root Binaries in PATH
Root-related binaries found in PATH directories.
Luna-method (psdir): Scanned getenv(PATH) directories for root binaries. Found: %1$s. Root binaries placed in PATH directories grant easy shell-level root access.
Remove root tools to eliminate these binaries from PATH.
No root binaries found in PATH directories.
Luna-method (psdir): No root-related binaries found when scanning getenv(PATH) directories.
APatch/Root Process Detected
APatch or root framework processes found running.
Luna-method (rustmagisk): /proc scan for APatch/root process names. Found: %1$s. apd is the APatch daemon; its presence confirms APatch is active. magiskd indicates Magisk is running.
Uninstall APatch via the APatch Manager app, or Magisk via Magisk Manager.
APatch/Root Process
No APatch or root framework processes detected.
Luna-method (rustmagisk): No APatch-related process names found in /proc.
Suspicious File Size Anomaly
System files have unexpected sizes, indicating possible tampering.
Luna-method (fhma): stat() check revealed files with anomalous sizes. %1$s. Root tools may replace or append to system files, inflating their size beyond expected.
Restore affected system files from a stock firmware image.
File Size Anomaly
No suspicious file size anomalies detected.
Luna-method (fhma): Checked system files for size anomalies; none found.
Kernel Stat Anomaly Detected
Kernel-level anomalies detected via /proc inspection.
Luna-method (checksuskernel): Kernel stat anomaly checks found: %1$s. Root frameworks register UNIX sockets in /proc/net/unix and may run with elevated SELinux contexts.
These kernel-level indicators are cleared when root frameworks are fully uninstalled.
Kernel Stat Anomaly
No kernel stat anomalies detected.
Luna-method (checksuskernel): No kernel anomalies found in /proc.
Magisk Mount Entries Detected
Magisk-related entries found in /proc/self/mounts.

Luna-method (magiskmounts): /proc/self/mounts contains characteristic Magisk bind-mount or overlay entries. Found: %1$s. Magisk hides itself and its modules by creating bind-mounts and overlay filesystems that leave traces in the mount table.
Uninstall Magisk via the Magisk Manager app and reboot to restore stock mounts.
Magisk Mount Entries
No Magisk-related mount entries found.
Luna-method (magiskmounts): /proc/self/mounts contains no known Magisk-specific bind-mount or overlay markers.
Zygote Injection Detected
SELinux context indicates possible Zygote-level injection by Magisk/LSPosed.

Luna-method (zygoteinject): SELinux context analysis found: %1$s. Magisk and LSPosed hook into Zygote to inject code into every new app process. This leaves characteristic SELinux context traces in /proc/self/attr/.
Uninstall Magisk and LSPosed, then verify SELinux policy is restored to stock.
Zygote Injection
No Zygote injection indicators found in SELinux contexts.
Luna-method (zygoteinject): /proc/self/attr/current and attr/prev show no signs of Zygote-level injection.
Tmpfs Mount Anomaly Detected
Suspicious tmpfs mounts detected on system mount points.

Native-Test-method (tmpfsmount): Anomalies found on /mnt/obb or /mnt/asec: %1$s. Magisk creates private tmpfs namespaces on these paths to isolate its module overlays from the global mount namespace, leaving multiple tmpfs entries for the same path.
Uninstall Magisk and reboot to restore stock mount namespaces.
Tmpfs Mount Anomaly
No suspicious tmpfs mount anomalies detected.
Native-Test-method (tmpfsmount): /mnt/obb and /mnt/asec mount entries appear normal.
Suspicious Processes / Root Overlay Mounts
Root framework processes or root-linked overlayfs mounts found.

Luna-method (procscan): /proc/<pid>/cmdline and /proc/self/mountinfo revealed: %1$s. Only overlays whose lowerdir/upperdir point to root-tool paths (e.g. /data/adb, /magisk) or that cover system paths outside APEX/RRO are flagged.
Uninstall root frameworks and reboot.
Process / Root Overlay Scan
No suspicious root processes or root-linked overlay mounts found.
Luna-method (procscan): No known root process names in /proc and no root-tool-linked overlayfs entries in mountinfo.
Root Binary / Framework Files Detected
Root-related files found on the filesystem.
Luna-method (roots): Root files found: %1$s.
Remove root tools to eliminate these files.
Root Binary / Framework Files
No root files found in standard locations.
Luna-method (roots): No root-related files found in common paths.
Custom / Rooted Kernel Detected
Kernel version or boot parameters indicate a custom/modified kernel.
Luna-method (kernels): %1$s. A custom kernel (e.g. KernelSU-patched) is required for kernel-level rooting.
Restore the stock kernel via Fastboot or supported recovery.
Kernel Version Check
Kernel version appears standard.
Luna-method (kernels): /proc/version contains no custom-kernel indicators.
Root Attestation File Detected
Root attestation test file found at /data/local/tmp/attestation.
Luna-method (findauth): access(/data/local/tmp/attestation) succeeded. Root tools create this file as an authorization/attestation marker.
Remove the file with root: `rm /data/local/tmp/attestation`.
Root Attestation File
No root attestation file found.
Luna-method (findauth): /data/local/tmp/attestation does not exist.
Root Native Modules Detected
Known root framework native libraries detected.
Luna-method (getEvilModules): Found: %1$s. libmagiskinit.so / libzygisk.so / liblspd.so are core root-framework components.
Uninstall the associated root framework.
Root Native Modules
No known root native module files detected.
Luna-method (getEvilModules): No known root native libraries found.
Root Management Apps Detected
Known root manager or hook framework apps are installed.
Luna-method (getapps): Installed root packages: %1$s.
Uninstall these applications to remove root indicators.
Root Management Apps
No known root management apps detected.
Luna-method (getapps): No known root manager or hook framework packages installed.
Automation Tool in Accessibility Services
Known root-automation tools are active as accessibility services.
Luna-method (findapply): Found suspicious accessibility services: %1$s. Auto.js and similar tools use accessibility APIs for root-based automation.
Disable the listed accessibility services in Settings.
Accessibility Service Check
No suspicious accessibility services detected.
Luna-method (findapply): No known automation tools found in enabled accessibility services.
Bootloader Unlocked
System properties indicate an unlocked bootloader.
Luna-method (findbootbl): %1$s. An unlocked bootloader is a prerequisite for flashing root frameworks.
Re-lock the bootloader via the manufacturer\'s official procedure.
Bootloader Status
Bootloader appears to be locked.
Luna-method (findbootbl): Boot properties indicate a locked, verified bootloader.
Vold Data Isolation Disabled
persist.sys.vold_app_data_isolation is set to 0 (disabled).
Luna-method (checknum): persist.sys.vold_app_data_isolation=0. Root tools disable vold app data isolation to gain cross-app storage access.
Re-enable storage data isolation; uninstall root tools that modify this property.
Vold Data Isolation
Vold data isolation property is not disabled.
Luna-method (checknum): persist.sys.vold_app_data_isolation=%1$s. Not disabled by root tools.
Root Native Libraries in App Dirs
Root framework native libraries found in installed app native-library directories.
Luna-method (scanlib): Found suspicious modules: %1$s.
Uninstall the associated app / root framework.
Native Library Scan
No root native libraries found in any installed app directory.
Luna-method (scanlib): No known root .so files found in nativeLibraryDir of any app.
Root-Hiding / Bypass Apps Detected
Apps designed to hide root presence or bypass detection are installed.
Luna-method (wNxM8s/K0ajGz): Found packages: %1$s. HideMyAppList and TaiChi conceal root-manager packages from app-level detection.
Uninstall these apps.
Root-Hiding / Bypass Apps
No root-hiding apps detected.
Luna-method (wNxM8s/K0ajGz): No known root-hiding packages installed.
MAC Address Anomaly (Zero MAC)
Network interface has a zero MAC address, indicating possible root spoofing.
Luna-method (magiskmac): %1$s. Magisk or root modules may suppress the MAC address to bypass device fingerprinting.
Investigate whether a Magisk module is suppressing MAC addresses.
MAC Address Check
No zero MAC address anomalies detected.
Luna-method (magiskmac): All readable network interfaces have non-zero MAC addresses.
Custom ROM Detected
A custom ROM (e.g. LineageOS) was detected on this device.
Custom ROM indicators found: %1$s. Custom ROMs may have relaxed security policies and often ship with root access enabled.
Flash the official OEM firmware via fastboot to restore a stock environment.
Custom ROM
No custom ROM detected.
No known custom ROM properties or fingerprint keywords found.
Custom Kernel Detected
A non-stock (custom) kernel was detected.
Custom kernel indicators in /proc/version: %1$s. Custom kernels may have security patches removed or extra capabilities enabled.
Flash the OEM stock kernel/boot image to restore an official kernel.
Custom Kernel
No custom kernel indicators found.
The kernel banner does not contain known custom-kernel strings.
Resetprop Detected
The resetprop tool was found on this device.
resetprop is a Magisk utility that can modify Android system properties at runtime without leaving traces in the normal property service. Found at: %1$s.
Uninstall Magisk via the Magisk Manager app or flash a stock boot image.
Resetprop
Resetprop tool not found.
No resetprop binary was detected at known Magisk paths.
Debug Fingerprint Detected
The device is running a userdebug or eng build.
Build type \'%1$s\' is a debug variant. Debug builds have ADB root, relaxed SELinux, and may expose sensitive system interfaces. Fingerprint: %2$s.
Use a production (\'user\') build for proper security hardening.
Debug Fingerprint
No debug fingerprint detected.
Build type is \'%1$s\', which is a production build.
Hide My Applist Detected
Hide My Applist framework detected.
Hide My Applist (HMA) is an Xposed/LSPosed module that intercepts PackageManager calls to hide installed apps from other applications. Indicators: %1$s.
Disable or uninstall the Hide My Applist module via LSPosed Manager.
Hide My Applist
Hide My Applist not detected.
No Hide My Applist packages or sockets were found.
Mount Inconsistency Detected
Mount table inconsistency detected — possible umount-based hiding.
Mounts visible in /proc/self/mountinfo but absent from /proc/mounts suggest a process has called umount() to hide root-related bind mounts from simpler mount file parsers. Suspicious paths: %1$s.
This is a strong indicator of Magisk or a similar framework using mount namespace manipulation. Uninstall the root framework.
Mount Inconsistency
No mount table inconsistency detected.
/proc/mounts and /proc/self/mountinfo are consistent.
Mount inconsistency check could not be completed.
Failed to read /proc/mounts or /proc/self/mountinfo.
Addon.d / install-recovery.sh Detected
OTA persistence scripts found in the system partition.
addon.d scripts and install-recovery.sh are used by custom ROMs and rooting frameworks to survive OTA updates by re-applying patches after a system update. Indicators: %1$s.
These files indicate a modified system. Flash an official OEM ROM to remove them.
Addon.d / install-recovery.sh
No addon.d scripts or install-recovery.sh found.
No OTA persistence scripts were detected in the system partition.
System Apps Hidden
Abnormally few system apps visible — app-list hiding likely active.
Only %1$d system app(s) returned by PackageManager. Normal Android devices expose 50+ system apps. This strongly indicates an app-list hiding framework (e.g. Hide My Applist) is intercepting queries.
Disable the app-list hiding module via LSPosed Manager.
System Apps Visibility
System apps appear normally visible.
%1$d system apps returned by PackageManager — within normal range.
Could not query system app list.
PackageManager query failed.
Vendor SEPolicy Lineage Check
vendor_sepolicy.cil not found or not accessible.
The vendor SELinux policy file was not present or not readable.
LineageOS SEPolicy Detected
vendor_sepolicy.cil contains LineageOS-specific entries.
The vendor SELinux policy file contains custom ROM policy entries: %1$s. This confirms a custom ROM build.
Flash an official OEM ROM to restore the original SELinux policy.
Vendor SEPolicy Lineage Check
No LineageOS entries in vendor_sepolicy.cil.
The vendor SELinux policy does not contain known custom ROM entries.
Could not read vendor_sepolicy.cil.
Permission denied or I/O error reading the SELinux policy file.
Framework Patch Check
/system/framework not accessible.
The framework directory could not be read.
No files found in /system/framework.
The framework directory appears empty.
Framework Patch Detected
System framework may have been patched.
Evidence of framework modification: %1$s. Xposed / LSPosed work by patching the Android runtime framework (services.jar, framework.jar) to allow hooking.
Remove the Xposed framework and restore the original framework files by flashing a stock ROM.
Framework Patch
No framework patch indicators found.
No suspicious files or modification time anomalies in /system/framework.
Could not complete framework patch check.
Permission denied or I/O error reading /system/framework.
Dual / Work Profile Detected
App is running in a clone/dual-space or work profile.

The app\'s filesDir is \'%1$s\', which starts with /data/user but not /data/user/0. This indicates the app is running in a secondary user, device-clone space, or Android work profile rather than the primary user space.
This is expected in dual-space or work-profile scenarios. Verify this is the intended execution environment.
Dual / Work Profile
Running in the primary user space (no dual/work profile detected).
filesDir=\'%1$s\' starts with /data/user/0, indicating the primary user.
XPrivacyLua Data Directory Detected
XPrivacyLua data directory found at /data/system/xlua.

XPrivacyLua is an Xposed module that fakes or restricts privacy-sensitive data returned to apps. Its presence at /data/system/xlua strongly indicates an active Xposed/LSPosed environment with privacy hooking.
Disable or remove the XPrivacyLua module via LSPosed Manager.
XPrivacyLua Data Directory
XPrivacyLua data directory not found.
No XPrivacyLua data directory detected at /data/system/xlua.
Xposed Edge Data Directory Detected
Xposed Edge data directory found at /data/system/xedge.
Xposed Edge is an Xposed module for advanced edge-gesture customization. Its data directory at /data/system/xedge indicates an active Xposed framework.
Disable the Xposed Edge module if not intentionally installed.
Xposed Edge Data Directory
Xposed Edge data directory not found.
No Xposed Edge data directory detected at /data/system/xedge.
Riru Clipboard Directory Detected
Riru Clipboard module data directory found at /data/misc/clipboard.
The Riru Clipboard Zygisk module leaves its data at /data/misc/clipboard. This directory indicates Riru or a related Zygisk module is (or was) installed.
Remove or disable the Riru Clipboard module if not intentionally installed.
Riru Clipboard Directory
Riru Clipboard data directory not found.
No Riru Clipboard directory found at /data/misc/clipboard.
Privacy Space Data Directory Detected
Privacy Space (cn.geektang.privacyspace) data directory found.

Privacy Space is an Xposed module that hides installed packages by intercepting PackageManager APIs. Its data directory at /data/system/cn.geektang.privacyspace confirms it is installed and likely active.
Disable or remove the Privacy Space module via LSPosed Manager.
Privacy Space Data Directory
Privacy Space data directory not found.
No Privacy Space directory found at /data/system/cn.geektang.privacyspace.
HMA (Old Version) Data Directory Detected
Hide My Applist old-version data directory found at /data/misc/hide_my_applist.
Older versions of the Hide My Applist Xposed module stored configuration data in /data/misc/hide_my_applist. The presence of this directory strongly suggests HMA was or is installed.
Uninstall Hide My Applist and clean up residual data.
HMA (Old Version) Data Directory
HMA old-version data directory not found.
No old-version HMA directory found at /data/misc/hide_my_applist.
PM Cross-Method Anomaly
Could not run `pm list packages` shell command.
The shell command `pm list packages` was unavailable or returned empty results. Cannot compare results against the PackageManager API.
Ensure the app has permission to execute shell commands.
PM Cross-Method Anomaly Detected
PackageManager API is hiding packages visible via shell.

The following packages appear in `pm list packages` shell output but are invisible to getInstalledPackages() API, indicating a PackageManager hook (e.g. HideMyApplist) is filtering API results. Hidden packages: %1$s.
Disable any Xposed/LSPosed module that hooks PackageManager (e.g. HideMyApplist, Privacy Space).
PM Cross-Method Anomaly
No discrepancy found between shell pm and PackageManager API.
Shell `pm list packages` and getInstalledPackages() API return consistent results for the checked package list.
Xposed Modules Detected via Metadata
Apps with Xposed module metadata found.
The following installed apps declare \'xposedminversion\' or \'xposeddescription\' metadata keys, identifying them as Xposed modules: %1$s.
Disable or uninstall identified Xposed modules via LSPosed Manager.
Xposed Module Metadata Scan
No apps with Xposed module metadata found.
No installed app declares \'xposedminversion\' or \'xposeddescription\' metadata.
LSPatch via AppComponentFactory
Check requires API 28+; skipped on this device.
The appComponentFactory attribute is only available on Android 9 (API 28)+.
LSPatch via AppComponentFactory Detected
Apps patched with LSPatch detected via appComponentFactory.

The following apps have an appComponentFactory attribute containing \'lsposed\' or \'lspatch\', indicating they were patched using LSPatch (an Xposed implementation that embeds the framework into APKs directly): %1$s.
Replace LSPatch-embedded APKs with original versions from official sources.
LSPatch via AppComponentFactory
No LSPatch-patched apps detected via appComponentFactory.
No app\'s appComponentFactory attribute contains \'lsposed\' or \'lspatch\'.
Account List Anomaly
Device accounts found via AccountManager.
AccountManager reports %1$d account(s) on this device. Some privacy-invasive apps or frameworks register fake accounts. Accounts: %2$s.
Review listed accounts and remove any suspicious ones via device Settings → Accounts.
No device accounts found in AccountManager.
AccountManager returned no accounts.
VPN Connection Detected
An active VPN or proxy connection was detected.
A VPN or proxy connection is active on this device. VPNs can be used to intercept network traffic or route it through third-party servers. Indicators: %1$s.
Disconnect any VPN or proxy if it is not intentionally configured.
VPN Connection
No VPN or proxy connection detected.
No VPN network interfaces (tun0/ppp0), TYPE_VPN connectivity, or http.proxyHost system property were found.
Accessibility Services Active
One or more accessibility services are currently enabled.

Accessibility services have broad system access and can read screen contents, simulate taps, and intercept input events. This may pose a security concern if unknown services are active. AccessibilityManager.isEnabled=%1$s, Services: %2$s.
Review enabled accessibility services in Settings → Accessibility and disable any you do not recognize.
Accessibility Services
No accessibility services are currently active.
AccessibilityManager.isEnabled=false and no enabled accessibility services found.
Package Manager API Discrepancy Detected
PackageManager APIs return inconsistent results for the same packages.

Discrepancies found between getApplicationInfo and sundry PM APIs (getPackageUid / getInstallSourceInfo / getLaunchIntentForPackage). This is the hallmark signature of an incomplete PackageManager hook (e.g. HideMyApplist): it intercepts some APIs but misses others. Discrepancies: %1$s.
Disable any Xposed/LSPosed modules that hook PackageManager.
Package Manager API Discrepancy
PackageManager APIs return consistent results.
getApplicationInfo and sundry PM APIs (getPackageUid, getInstallSourceInfo, getLaunchIntentForPackage) agree on visibility for all checked packages.
Failed: %1$s
Failed to read %1$s: %2$s
Error: %1$s
%1$s query failed: %2$s
